schools.summary: Textual variants Gadarenes/Gerasenes/Gergesenes harmonized (Lightfoot: Gadara the heathen city within the wider Gergesene region); the demoniac met Christ across the sea.
The demons confess Christ (refuting the Pharisees' charge); the demoniac read as the Gentile peoples freed from Satan (Bede); demons hurt none without divine permission (Remigius).
Henry: Christ crosses a storm to redeem a remnant from Satan; the Gadarenes prefer their swine to their Saviour — carnal hearts misread God's judgments.
Mark's vivid 'straightway'; he and Luke focus on the one more violent demoniac, Matthew notes two — emphasis, not contradiction (Augustine, Henry); the man dwells among the tombs.

themes: a man with an unclean spirit

schools.summary: He dwelt among the tombs and snapped every chain; the attempts to bind him had been given up in despair (Ellicott).
Calvin: Satan is sometimes permitted preternatural strength beyond nature — a shocking reminder of how wretched it is to be under his tyranny; soul-distress worse than bodily agony.
Every chain and fetter broken (vivid perfect tenses, perhaps eyewitness fragments); none could tame him — Mark's characteristic fulness of detail.
Clarke: his supernatural strength proves real diabolic agency, against those who reduce it to mere madness — the inspired writers must be credited.
Ceaseless screeching and self-gashing night and day among tombs and mountains (a detail only in Mark) — the signature of demonic torment.
Clarke: a specimen of Satan's cruelty to all the wicked if permitted — yet the devil himself has his chain; Henry: the voice of Satan is 'do thyself all the harm thou canst.'
The raging man runs and bows — homage compelled by Christ's power (Clarke); the onrushing maniac throws himself on his knees, surprising the Twelve (Robertson).
Calvin: the demons are dragged reluctantly before their judge by Christ's secret authority — the whole of Satan's kingdom is subject to him.
'Son of the Most High God' (first NT use; Genesis 14:18) — a name used in exorcism formulae, now forced from the demoniac's lips; 'I adjure thee' is the exorcist's word turned to entreaty (Ellicott).
Cyril: the demons themselves are among the many true witnesses to Christ — 'we know Thee, who Thou art.'

themes: Son of the most high God

schools.summary: The imperfect 'he was saying' shows Christ repeatedly commanded the spirit out, provoking the demon's plea; he speaks first as to one spirit before the Legion is revealed (Ellicott).
A Roman legion ~6,000 men (here perhaps fewer, ~2,000 hogs); Christ asks not from ignorance but to display the man's misery and his own power (Gill).
Calvin: the demon is forced to confess his multitude, magnifying Christ's grace; refutes the error of one private devil per man — many devils may punish one man.
Henry: the legion is military, numerous, unanimous (refuting 'Satan divided'), powerful, and ordered — yet we stand in the Lord's might.
The demons beg to stay in the country; they cannot move without Christ's leave (Gill); the demoniac half-identifies with the forces tormenting him (Ellicott).
Calvin: they dread being sent to the deep because it deprives them of their prey — their whole desire is the destruction of mankind, yet they must beg Christ's permission.

themes: not send them out of the country

schools.summary: A great herd of swine near the mountains confirms the heathen setting (swine forbidden to Jews, Leviticus 11:7); the unclean animal a fit refuge for the unclean spirits (Remigius, Henry).
The whole legion begs to enter the swine — supplicants from fear, not love, aiming at further mischief (Henry); they cannot act without Christ's leave (cf. Job 1:12).
Mark's precise '~two thousand'; the difficulty of why Christ permitted it — better hogs than men, yet a real loss of property (Robertson).
The permission displayed publicly the murderous fury of the demons toward men, which divine power had restrained — furthering the salvation of men (Pseudo-Chrysostom, Bede).
The terrified herdsmen flee and spread the report through city and country (Mark adds 'in the fields'); the loss of the swine draws the whole region out to see.
The once-raging man now sits clothed and sane at Jesus' feet — undeniable proof of divine power; the people are awed (Barnes); Mark's antithesis 'had the legion.'
Calvin: their fear was awe at God's majesty in Christ, yet did not ripen to faith — those who think the kingdom against their interest have no relish for grace.

themes: sitting, clothed, in his right mind

schools.summary: Gill: the eyewitnesses (not the fled herdsmen) report both the deliverance and the loss of the swine to the crowd — laying the full evidence before them, leaving the country without excuse.

themes: they that saw it told them

schools.summary: The Gadarenes beg Christ to leave, prizing their swine above their Saviour — business over spiritual welfare (Robertson); the love of the world drives away the Deliverer (Henry).
The delivered man begs to go with Christ — in pointed contrast to the country's rejection; the rescued soul covets fellowship with its Deliverer (Henry).

themes: that he might be with him

schools.summary: Christ sends him home to witness rather than follow — fitting for Decapolis where there was no danger of excess enthusiasm (Robertson); 'the Lord' = the Father (Ellicott, cf. Luke 8:39).
Henry: he is to glory above all in Christ's compassion, not merely his power — sent to witness where he is best known.

themes: tell how great things the Lord hath done

schools.summary: The man obeys, heralding throughout Decapolis 'how great things Jesus had done' — the first gospel witness there, his changed life itself a testimony; all kept marvelling (Robertson).
Theophylact: Christ said 'tell what the Lord hath done' to teach humility — yet the man names Jesus as that Lord, a quiet testimony to his deity.

themes: how great things Jesus had done

schools.summary: Bede/Pseudo-Jerome: the woman healed = the Gentile Church by faith's touch; the ruler's daughter raised after = Israel saved when the fulness of the Gentiles comes in (Romans 11).
Theophylact: Jairus only partly faithful (begs Christ to come, not just speak); the woman most faithful, hoping for healing from his garment.
Henry: the two miracles intertwined; people come to Christ only as a last refuge, yet he is a sure refuge even to those who make him their last.
A ruler of the synagogue (the council president) falls at Jesus' feet — a striking humility born of his dying daughter's need; the named Jairus marks the miracle as attested (Pseudo-Chrysostom).

outliers.note: The JFB fragment here, on the rending of the temple veil, belongs to Matthew 27, not this verse.

schools.summary: The tender plea — 'my little daughter' (Mark's affectionate diminutive), at the point of death (in extremis); a real if partial faith that Christ's touch will heal her.
Christ promptly went with Jairus (without rebuking his weak faith) while the crowd thronged and pressed him (imperfect tenses) — the press providentially cover for the woman's secret touch.
The woman's twelve-year issue of blood was debilitating and ceremonially defiling (Leviticus 15:19); chronic and incurable, setting the stage for the instant cure (Henry).
Twelve years of fruitless, costly recourse to physicians (the rabbinic remedies, some useless or harmful); 'nothing bettered, but rather grew worse' — her extremity God's opportunity (Clarke).
She presses through the crowd and touches his garment from behind — faith mingled with shy, conscious unworthiness (Barnes); her uncleanness made her shrink from an open approach (Chrysostom).
Her inward resolution of faith — 'if I may touch but his clothes' — without precedent grasped the inherent healing power of Christ (Henry); a secret cure suited to a secret grief.

outliers.note: The JFB fragment here, on the women at the cross, belongs to Matthew 27, not this verse.

themes: if I may touch his clothes

schools.summary: The issue dried up 'straightway'; she felt in her body she was healed (Mark's graphic touch) of her 'plague' (scourge) — an instant, complete, miraculous cure (Henry).

Christ consciously felt the 'virtue' (supernatural power) go out at the touch of faith; he asks 'who touched me' not to learn but to draw the woman forward to confess (Barnes) — faith's touch distinct from the crowd's press.

The disciples think the question needless in a pressing crowd; but many touch Christ without faith and receive nothing, while faith alone draws his healing virtue (Clarke) — the throng's press distinct from the faith-touch.
Christ keeps looking round (continued action) to find the woman — not to inform himself but to draw her forward and confirm her cure (Henry); his answer to the disciples' protest.
The woman, trembling with needless fear, falls down and confesses 'all the truth' — the open avowal of her secret faith; her groundless fear mirrors the trembling believer (Gill, Henry).
The tender 'Daughter'; 'thy faith hath made thee whole' (faith grasping Christ's power, not the touch); 'go in(to) peace' (shalom, health of body and soul) — the cure confirmed by his word.
Calvin: the benefit is fully ratified when she hears from Christ's lips what she learned by experience — we enjoy God's benefits as contained in the treasury of his promises.

outliers.note: The JFB fragment here, on the women at the cross, belongs to Matthew 27, not this verse.

themes: thy faith hath made thee whole
the cure ratified by his word

schools.summary: The messengers (and the world) think Christ's power, like a physician's, reaches only the living (Clarke); 'why trouble the Master?' — but he raised the widow's son at Nain.
To men she is dead, to God asleep (Bede; whence Christians call the dead 'asleep'); the daughter = the synagogue raised after the Gentile Church (the matching twelve years).
Henry: faith the only remedy against grief — 'only believe,' believe the resurrection; the mourners' scorn proves she was truly dead.
Christ overhears the message of death, ignores its despairing conclusion, and steadies the father: 'Be not afraid, only believe' — spoken to prevent his distress (Clarke).
Calvin: faith must not shut out grace by fear, for death is no hindrance to Christ; our narrow desires, not his limited power, straiten his gifts — we cannot believe beyond bounds.
Only Peter, James, and John — the inner three (also at the Transfiguration and Gethsemane); enough to attest the miracle, not so many as to savour of vainglory (Henry).
Calvin: the crowd excluded lest the miracle be overpowered by noise or display; Chrysostom: he leaves witnesses of his divine power while avoiding ostentation.
Christ meets the tumult of noisy Eastern mourning — wailing relatives and hired mourners with clanging, inarticulate cries (the alala of cymbals); the din contrasts with Christ's calm.
Christ stills the tumult: 'not dead, but sleepeth' — not denying her death but declaring he will wake her (cf. Lazarus, John 11:11); a beautiful word for death (Robertson).
'Dead to you, asleep to Me' (Pseudo-Jerome, Bede) — whence Christians call the dead 'asleep'; in God's purpose the flesh rests to rise again.
Calvin: he distinguishes death and sleep to excite expectation of life; the scorn confirms she was truly dead, magnifying the miracle.
The mourners jeer (kept it up) — proving her real death; Christ, as master of the house, puts them all out and admits only the parents and the three (Robertson).
Bede: the scoffers, choosing scorn over belief, are excluded as unworthy; mystically, the crowd of worldly thoughts must be cast out before the dead soul can rise.

outliers.note: The JFB fragment here, on the sealed tomb, belongs to Matthew 27, not this verse.

schools.summary: Mark preserves the Aramaic 'Talitha cumi' (Peter's memory); the touch of his hand a 'touch of life'; not a physician's wish but a sovereign command carrying power to accomplish it (Lightfoot, Henry).
Calvin: a magnificent display of the power of Christ's voice, which reaches even the dead; Chrysostom: his hand and voice have quickening power.

The child instantly rose and walked in full health (the walking proving the cure complete); 'twelve years old' (Mark alone, at the close) marks independent eyewitness detail; all overwhelmed with astonishment.

The charge to silence reserves the full display of power over death for Christ's own resurrection (Henry); the food proves the cure complete and teaches that the miraculous does not dispense with ordinary means (Clarke).
Gill: silence to avoid ostentation and the Pharisees' envy; Christ stoops to see the raised child fed — extraordinary power joined to ordinary care.
